gold.rationale: The article is built around a misleading escalation: a real Shenzhen consultation on traffic management for e-bikes is reframed as a broad citywide ban. Its visuals further intensify that false takeaway by using synthetic and edited imagery that depicts blanket prohibition and active enforcement not supported by the source evidence.

atomic_points.post_summary: The post reports that Shenzhen traffic police released a draft proposal on Feb. 11, 2026, to ban electric bicycles on city roads, with public comments open until March 12, citing safety and delivery fleet pressures.

paragraph_level: Shenzhen is implementing a draft policy to ban electric bicycles on city roads.
The draft proposal was released by Shenzhen traffic police on Feb. 11, 2026.
Public comment on the proposal is open until March 12, 2026.
The policy aims to restrict non-motor vehicle access and enforce road space usage.
Visual materials associated with the proposal include a 'no-e-bike' symbol and the text 'E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE'.
The draft reclassifies road access, adjusting restrictions for some routes and removing them for others.
The policy shift is driven by pressures from road safety, school-zone traffic, and expanding delivery fleets in Chinese cities.

image_1 sub-headline states 'Unregistered electric bicycles are prohibited from driving on the road'.

image_1 sign text indicates implementation date 'September 1, 2023' and issuer 'Shenzhen Public Security Bureau Traffic Police Brigade'.
image_1 background shows a cyclist riding past a parking sign labeled 'Non-motor vehicle parking area'.

image_2 sign features a red circle with a slash over a bicycle icon.
image_2 sign text reads 'Electric Bicycles Citywide Ban' and 'E-bikes Prohibited Citywide'.
image_2 sign states implementation time 'August 1, 2025'.
image_2 shows a police officer wearing a vest labeled 'Shenzhen Traffic Police' and 'SHENZHEN POLICE'.
image_2 shows a delivery rider wearing a yellow vest with 'Meituan Waimai' (Meituan Delivery) logo.
image_2 background includes a blue road sign with directions to 'Shennan Blvd' and 'Hua Fu Rd'.

retrieved_evidence.text: Shenzhen’s dense urban streetscapes feature a mix of private cars, buses, pedestrians and large numbers of electric bicycle riders, many of whom work in app-based food delivery and courier services. In recent reports, local police officers have been described as stepping up roadside inspections and directing riders near intersections where prominent signs warn against e-bike access. According to transport specialists, if the proposed Shenzhen traffic rules evolve into a full ban on electric bicycles on city roads, everyday commuters and delivery workers could face longer travel times, higher costs and a forced shift toward public transport or other approved vehicles.

【“深圳将禁行电动自行车”不实】深圳市公安局交通管理局2月11日发布《深圳市公安局交通管理局关于对电动自行车等非机动车实施通行管理措施的通告(征求意见稿)》，意见征求时间截至2026年3月12日。部分网民浏览自媒体消息时误认为“深圳电动自行车要禁行了”。实际上，深圳电动自行车限行政策此次拟调整的仅是几条道路的限行等级与删除部分限行道路。限行规则没有变化，和以前是一样的。如果不涉及调整的道路，则根本没有影响。（来源：@南方日报）

The city’s traffic police released a draft notice on February 11, 2026 outlining proposed management measures for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to information published on official platforms. The document, described by police representatives as a preliminary consultation paper, sets out suggested rules for how these vehicles may use urban roads and related facilities. Early reports indicate that the draft was prepared by traffic management authorities following internal assessments of congestion and safety issues, and that further revisions could be made after collecting feedback from residents, industry groups and other stakeholders.

The draft regulation circulated in recent weeks by the city’s traffic police focuses on road access management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, according to officials briefed on the document. The proposal reportedly sets out which categories of roads such vehicles may enter, the time periods for use in certain busy areas, and possible separation from motor traffic in some sections. Authorities say the goal is to refine existing arrangements rather than introduce a comprehensive new framework, though specific implementation details are still subject to further technical discussion.

According to local transportation authorities, the draft notice on traffic management for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles was made available for public comment until March 12, 2026. Officials said members of the public were invited to submit opinions through online systems and other designated channels during this period, with the aim of incorporating reasonable suggestions into a revised version of the measure. People familiar with the process noted that comments would be sorted and analyzed by the traffic police and relevant departments before any final decision is taken.

In Shenzhen, a recently circulated traffic management proposal has drawn attention by outlining stricter rules for electric bicycles and signaling a potential citywide ban on their use on urban roads. According to local transport authorities, such regulatory drafts are typically issued as formal notices carrying the municipal emblem, standardized fonts and legal references, and are used to inform residents, logistics companies and employers about upcoming changes in commuting and delivery practices. Observers note that this latest proposal appears to extend existing restrictions into a broader crackdown on e-bike commuting across Shenzhen’s main districts.

Traffic management departments said in recent statements that the current restriction framework for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles remains largely intact, with no fundamental overhaul of the existing rules. According to officials, only roads explicitly listed in the adjustment plan are expected to see changes, while other routes will continue to follow the original access provisions. Observers noted that this means most daily travel patterns for riders are unlikely to be affected in the short term, though authorities may revisit the scheme based on future evaluations and data.

In an official clarification issued recently, traffic authorities stated that the main policy adjustment under discussion concerns changing restriction levels on only a limited number of roads while lifting restrictions on some previously controlled sections. According to people familiar with the explanation, the revised plan would ease constraints in certain corridors considered suitable for electric bicycles and other non-motor vehicles, while maintaining or slightly tightening rules on a small set of busy routes. Officials emphasized that the announced changes are targeted in scope and would be implemented gradually if approved.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post claims a citywide ban and uses images with future dates (2025) and past dates (2023) to support a 2026 draft, while evidence clarifies the draft only adjusts specific road restrictions, not a total ban.
text_analysis.t2_evidence: The post states 'Shenzhen is moving toward a ban on electric bicycles on city roads' and describes the proposal as signaling a 'much tougher line', whereas evidence [1] explicitly states 'Shenzhen will ban electric bicycles' is untrue and the draft only adjusts restriction levels for a few roads while removing restrictions on others.
t2_problematic_subclaims: Shenzhen is moving toward a ban on electric bicycles on city roads.
The draft notice signals a much tougher line on non-motor vehicle access.
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post presents image_2 (dated '2025年8月1日' / Aug 1, 2025) and image_1 (dated '2023-08-31') as visual evidence for a draft proposal released on Feb 11, 2026, creating a temporal mismatch where the images depict either a future hypothetical enforcement or a past regulation, not the current 2026 draft.
t3_problematic_subclaims: A lead image tied to the proposal uses police-style enforcement imagery...
Another city-street visual features the bold bilingual message: “E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE,” matching the harder tone of the consultation.
text_analysis.c1_evidence: The post text claims the proposal was released on Feb 11, 2026, but image_2 contains OCR text '实施时间：2025年8月1日' (Implementation Date: Aug 1, 2025), and image_1 contains OCR text '2023-08-31', contradicting the post's temporal framing of the current draft.
c1_problematic_subclaims: Shenzhen traffic police released the proposal on Feb. 11, 2026... Another city-street visual features the bold bilingual message: “E-BIKES BANNED CITYWIDE”
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post significantly distorts the scope of the policy (claiming a ban vs. minor adjustments) and uses temporally inconsistent images to mislead readers about the nature and timing of the regulation.

The image includes a photo of a blue traffic sign announcing a ban on e-bikes starting September 1, 2023.

images.v2_evidence: The image is a screenshot of a news report dated August 31, 2023, regarding regulations effective September 1, 2023, which contradicts the post's claim that the image illustrates a 'draft proposal' released in February 2026.
images.c_evidence: The image depicts a finalized regulation from 2023, whereas the post text claims the image supports a 'draft proposal' released in 2026, creating a significant temporal and status mismatch.
images.explanation: The image is a real screenshot but is contextually mismatched with the post's timeline, showing a 2023 regulation instead of the claimed 2026 draft.

images.scene_description: A street scene showing Shenzhen traffic police officers interacting with delivery riders. A prominent sign in the foreground displays a 'No E-bikes' symbol and text stating 'E-bikes Prohibited Citywide' with an implementation date of 'August 1, 2025'.
images.ocr_text: 电动自行车 全市禁行 | E-bikes Prohibited Citywide | 实施时间：2025年8月1日 | 深圳公安 | 深圳交警 SHENZHEN POLICE | 买果切 上美团外卖 | 交通管制 TRAFFIC CONTROL
images.v1_evidence: The image exhibits high-fidelity AI generation artifacts, including the 'too perfect' lighting, uniform sharpness, and the presence of a specific future date (2025) on a sign that does not exist in reality, characteristic of AI-generated press-style imagery.
images.c_evidence: The sign in the image displays a date of 'August 1, 2025', which contradicts the post's claim that the image illustrates a 'draft proposal' released in 'February 2026'.
images.explanation: The image is likely AI-generated synthetic content depicting a fictional future ban date (2025) that conflicts with the post's narrative about a 2026 draft.

image_analysis.global_notes: Both images are mismatched with the post's specific claims about a 2026 draft proposal. Image 1 is a real screenshot from 2023, and Image 2 appears to be AI-generated with a fictional 2025 date.

judge.level3_rationale: The post falsely claims Shenzhen is moving toward a citywide e-bike ban, whereas evidence confirms the draft only adjusts specific road restrictions; additionally, the supporting images are misleading, with one being from a 2023 report and the other appearing synthetic with a mismatched 2025 date.
judge.reasoning_trace: The core claim of a 'ban' is contradicted by the fact check. The images are temporally inconsistent with the text (2023 and 2025 vs 2026) and one is likely synthetic. This triggers T2, T3, V1, V2, C1, C2. With 6 labels and V1 present, the verdict is False.
judge.core_claim: Shenzhen traffic police released a draft proposal on Feb. 11, 2026, to ban electric bicycles on city roads, supported by images of enforcement and ban signs.

The proposal aims to ban electric bicycles on city roads.
The proposal is open for public comment until March 12, 2026.
The proposal involves reclassifying some roads and adjusting restriction levels.
Image 1 is tied to the 2026 proposal and shows a ban implemented in 2023.
Image 2 shows a sign for the 2026 proposal with a ban date of August 1, 2025.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Shenzhen traffic police released a draft proposal on Feb. 11, 2026.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the release date and the existence of the draft.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The proposal aims to ban electric bicycles on city roads.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence explicitly states the 'citywide ban' claim is false; the draft only adjusts specific road restrictions.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The proposal is open for public comment until March 12, 2026.
The proposal involves reclassifying some roads and adjusting restriction levels.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the draft focuses on adjusting restriction levels on a limited number of roads.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Image 1 is tied to the 2026 proposal and shows a ban implemented in 2023.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 1 is a screenshot of a 2023 news report, not the 2026 draft.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Image 2 shows a sign for the 2026 proposal with a ban date of August 1, 2025.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 2 shows a sign with a 2025 date, which contradicts the 2026 timeline and is likely synthetic or misattributed.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post contains a mix of true details (dates of the draft) and false core claims (citywide ban). The core claim of a 'ban' is explicitly contradicted by evidence, which states it is a targeted adjustment. The images are misleading: Image 1 is from a different event (2023), and Image 2 is likely synthetic with a mismatched date (2025). The presence of T2 (distortion of policy scope), V1 (synthetic visual), and multiple cross-modal inconsistencies (C1, C2) results in a high count of L2 labels (6). According to the rules, 6+ L2 labels with a fabrication label (V1) mandates a 'False' verdict.

image_findings.explanation: The image is a screenshot of a 2023 news report, used out of context to support a 2026 draft proposal claim.
The image appears to be AI-generated or heavily edited, featuring a sign with a 2025 date that does not match the 2026 draft timeline or the actual policy content.
